title: Payment of Gratuity Act 1972 — Eligibility and Calculation
content: Under Section 4 of the Payment of Gratuity Act 1972, gratuity is payable to an employee on termination of service after completing a minimum of 5 years of continuous service. The 5-year rule is waived in cases of death or disability. Calculation formula: (Last Drawn Monthly Salary × 15 × Years of Service) ÷ 26. 'Last drawn salary' means basic wages plus dearness allowance. The maximum gratuity payable is ₹20 Lakhs. Gratuity is payable within 30 days of becoming due; the employer must pay simple interest at 10% per annum for any delay.

title: Gratuity Eligibility — 4 Years 8 Months Service (Madras HC)
content: The Madras High Court has held that an employee who completes 4 years and 240 days (approximately 4 years and 8 months) of service qualifies for gratuity, as 240 days constitutes a full year of service for establishments operating 6 days per week. For 5-day-week establishments, the threshold is 190 days in the fifth year. This judicial interpretation is widely followed and effectively reduces the practical threshold from 5 full calendar years.

title: Maternity Benefit (Amendment) Act 2017 — Leave Entitlement
content: Under the Maternity Benefit (Amendment) Act 2017, a female employee is entitled to 26 weeks of paid maternity leave for the first two live births, and 12 weeks for subsequent births. Eligibility requires 80 days of actual work in the 12 months preceding the expected delivery. The benefit applies to all establishments with 10 or more employees. Paid maternity leave also applies in cases of miscarriage (6 weeks), medical termination of pregnancy (6 weeks), and tubectomy (2 weeks). Employers with 50 or more employees must provide creche facilities.

title: Industrial Disputes Act 1947 — Section 25F Retrenchment Compensation
content: Under Section 25F of the Industrial Disputes Act 1947, an employer cannot retrench a workman who has completed one year of continuous service without: (a) giving one month's written notice or pay in lieu; (b) paying retrenchment compensation at 15 days' average pay for every completed year of service; and (c) giving notice to the appropriate government authority. Retrenchment without these steps is unlawful. An aggrieved employee can file a complaint before the Labour Court within 3 years of the retrenchment.

title: Industrial Disputes Act — Section 25G (Last In First Out Rule)
content: Section 25G of the Industrial Disputes Act 1947 requires that an employer retrenching workmen in any category must retrench the person most recently employed in that category first (last-in-first-out principle), unless there are valid reasons for departing from this order. Deviation from this rule without reasons constitutes unlawful discrimination in retrenchment.

title: Industrial Employment (Standing Orders) Act 1946 — Notice Period
content: Under the Industrial Employment (Standing Orders) Act 1946, certified standing orders for a factory or establishment must specify the notice period required for termination. For permanent workmen, the typical minimum notice period is 30 days. If the offer letter or contract specifies a longer notice period (e.g., 60 or 90 days), that contractual period applies. An employer who terminates without giving the agreed notice must pay wages in lieu of notice. An employee who resigns without serving notice may have pay deducted for the shortfall.

title: Factories Act 1948 — Section 51/59 Overtime Pay
content: Under the Factories Act 1948, no adult worker shall be required or permitted to work for more than 9 hours on any day or 48 hours in any week (Section 51). Under Section 59, if a worker works more than 9 hours a day or 48 hours a week, they must be paid overtime at twice the ordinary rate of wages. The overtime limit is 60 hours per week and 50 hours in any quarter. Violations are punishable under Section 92 of the Act.

title: Shops and Establishments Acts — Overtime for Non-Factory Workers
content: Workers in shops, commercial establishments, and offices are governed by the respective state Shops and Establishments Act. Most state acts cap daily working hours at 9 hours and weekly hours at 48 hours. Overtime beyond these limits must be compensated at 1.5x to 2x the ordinary rate, depending on the state act. Employers cannot compel workers to work overtime without their consent in most states.

title: POSH Act 2013 — Internal Complaints Committee Mandate
content: Under the Sexual Harassment of Women at Workplace (Prevention, Prohibition and Redressal) Act 2013, every employer with 10 or more employees is required to constitute an Internal Complaints Committee (ICC). The ICC must have a presiding officer who is a senior female employee, at least two other employees, and one member from an NGO or women's association. The ICC must complete enquiry within 60 days of receiving a complaint.

title: POSH Act 2013 — Local Complaints Committee for Small Establishments
content: For workplaces with fewer than 10 employees or where a complaint is against the employer directly, complaints are filed with the Local Complaints Committee (LCC) constituted by the District Officer. The LCC has the same powers as an ICC. Employees of unorganised sector workplaces and domestic workers can also file complaints with the LCC.

title: Code on Social Security 2020 — Gig and Platform Worker Coverage
content: The Code on Social Security 2020 consolidates 9 central labour laws including the EPF Act, ESI Act, Maternity Benefit Act, and Gratuity Act. Chapter IX of the Code specifically recognises gig workers (those engaged in task-based work outside the traditional employer-employee relationship) and platform workers (those working through digital platforms). The Code requires state governments to set up Social Security Funds for gig and platform workers, funded by contributions from aggregator platforms. Benefits include life and disability cover, accident insurance, and health and maternity benefits.

title: Industrial Relations Code 2020 — Retrenchment Threshold
content: The Industrial Relations Code 2020 consolidates the Industrial Disputes Act, the Trade Unions Act, and the Industrial Employment (Standing Orders) Act. Under the Code, establishments with 300 or more workers require prior government permission before retrenchment or closure. Establishments with fewer than 300 workers can retrench workers by following the notice and compensation provisions without seeking prior permission. This threshold was raised from 100 workers under the Industrial Disputes Act.

title: Relieving Letter — Employer's Obligation
content: There is no specific central statute mandating that an employer must issue a relieving letter. However, withholding a relieving letter as coercion or to prevent future employment may be challenged as restraint of trade under Section 27 of the Indian Contract Act 1872. An employee can approach the Labour Commissioner or file a civil suit for recovery of the relieving letter. Several High Courts have directed employers to issue relieving letters when withheld without valid cause.

title: Notice Period Buy-Out — Employee Resignation Before Notice Expires
content: When an employee resigns and is unable or unwilling to serve the full notice period specified in the employment contract or standing orders, the employer may allow a 'notice buy-out' — the employee pays the employer wages equivalent to the shortfall in notice days. The amount is typically calculated as (Basic + DA) ÷ 30 × remaining notice days. The employer cannot withhold the full and final settlement solely because of a notice period dispute; any recovery must be through civil proceedings or agreed deduction from F&F. An employee cannot be forced to serve the notice period against their will — specific performance of a service contract is barred under Section 14 of the Specific Relief Act 1963.

title: Notice Period Waiver — Employer's Right to Relieve Early
content: An employer may, at its discretion, waive the notice period and relieve the employee immediately or earlier than the contractual notice end date. In this case, the employer must pay the employee salary for the full notice period (pay in lieu of notice) unless the employee voluntarily agrees to forgo it. If the employer relieves the employee early without notice pay, the employee can recover the shortfall as unpaid wages under the Payment of Wages Act 1936 or through a civil suit. Early relieving by mutual consent is the cleanest route and should be documented in writing.

title: Wrongful Termination — Employee Remedies and Forum Selection
content: An employee who has been wrongfully terminated has several remedies depending on their category: (1) Workmen (as defined under the Industrial Disputes Act) can raise an industrial dispute before the Labour Commissioner, and if unresolved, before the Labour Court, which can award reinstatement with back wages. (2) Managerial or supervisory employees (not 'workmen') must approach civil courts or the High Court; they cannot use Labour Courts. (3) Employees in government or public sector establishments may have access to service tribunals or departmental appeals. Filing a complaint under the ID Act must be done within 3 years of the wrongful termination. If the employer is in an 'essential service', different notice requirements apply.

title: Termination for Misconduct — Effect on Gratuity
content: Under Section 4(6) of the Payment of Gratuity Act 1972, gratuity can be forfeited wholly or partially if an employee's services are terminated due to: (a) willful omission or negligence causing damage or loss to the employer's property; or (b) riotous or disorderly conduct or any other act of violence. Gratuity can also be wholly forfeited if the termination is due to an offence involving moral turpitude. Termination for ordinary misconduct (e.g., poor performance, insubordination without violence) does NOT forfeit gratuity — the employee remains entitled to gratuity if they have completed 5 years of service. The employer must follow a disciplinary inquiry and issue a charge sheet before forfeiting gratuity.

title: Garden Leave During Notice Period
content: Garden leave (also called 'gardening leave') is a practice where an employer asks an employee to stay away from the workplace during the notice period while continuing to pay their full salary. This is legally permissible if the employment contract or standing orders allow it. During garden leave, the employee's employment continues — they are still entitled to salary, benefits, and statutory entitlements such as EPF contributions. Garden leave clauses are common in senior roles and IT/financial sector contracts where the employer wants to prevent the employee from immediately joining a competitor. The employee cannot be compelled to work if placed on garden leave.
